Section 02 / 30 

Modernizing Holy Family University’s web presence. 

Small College Consulting is honored to present this proposal to Holy Family University. In partnership with the University’s team, we are confident that our approach will not only strengthen the institution’s website and connected marketing channels, but will also position University personnel to manage and evolve the site in the future with little to no external vendor support. Our intent is to deliver a powerful, modern website while minimizing the University’s future investment and providing a website management methodology that a small internal team can execute.

Members of the Small College Consulting team already have deep working relationships with members of the University’s leadership, and we developed the current chatbot on holyfamily.edu that is generating inquiries and informing content strategy today. We are confident that we are the right partners for this project and hope to deepen our existing relationships and expand the scope of our work together.

This proposal addresses the requirements of the RFP in full. The mapping table on page four is intentionally dense, allowing evaluators to locate the response to any individual requirement without searching.

Small College Consulting has also developed proprietary AI technology in preparation for this engagement. The technology facilitates seamless redesign and content migration from the existing Drupal installation into WordPress while simultaneously optimizing the new site for AI-engine visibility. It has been tested and fully validated against real institutional content.

An example of a quickly developed draft page modeled after Tufts University’s homepage. Our proprietary technology enables page redesigns to be informed by reference sites the University’s team wishes to draw from. 

The migration console used to translate content from the current Drupal installation into WordPress block patterns. AI drafts the classifications and block layout; the University’s editors review and approve every page.

Proposed information architecture 
Section 06 / 30 

Sitemap, drafted · 1,601 → ~650 pages

A first-pass IA for holyfamily.edu. 

The current site comprises 1,601 URLs distributed across more than 180 distinct top-level prefixes. The first-pass recommendation presented here consolidates that inventory into eight sections and approximately 650 active pages, a reduction of roughly sixty percent. Every URL is justified by a primary audience. We anticipate that a portion of this recommendation will evolve during Discovery; the remainder reflects structural decisions we believe will hold.

/ holyfamily.edu Target ~650 pages · from 1,601 

/programs/ academic programs hub ~130 pgs 

/programs/undergraduate/ 30+ majors ~45 pgs 

/programs/graduate/ 20+ grad programs ~40 pgs 

/programs/adult-continuing/ ACE + credit for prior learning ~25 pgs 

/programs/online/ online degree paths ~20 pgs 

/admissions/ enrollment hub · one smart RFI form ~45 pgs 

/student-life/ residential, clubs, ministry ~55 pgs 

/about/ mission, leadership, history ~40 pgs 

/news/ 2-year rolling window · 500+ archived ~120 pgs 

/athletics/ 19 varsity programs ~65 pgs 

/alumni/ giving, events, stories ~45 pgs 

/directory/ faculty & staff · single source ~150 pgs 

A sixty-percent reduction is a planning target, not a commitment to delete content. Roughly 500 news articles older than two years will move to an archive subdomain with their 301 redirects preserved. The twenty-three duplicate request-for-information forms will collapse into a single smart form routed to Slate by program. Duplicate program pages will consolidate into canonical parents with sub-tracks. A card sort and tree test will validate the remaining information architecture with real users before Phase 2 closes.

RFP Section B.3 · Information Architecture Approach · first-pass recommendation

The Philadelphia Catholic peer landscape 
Section 07 / 30 

Your neighborhood, on the web

Holy Family and its Philadelphia Catholic peers. 

The institutions most directly competing for the attention of a prospective Holy Family student on any given visit weekend include La Salle, Villanova, and Neumann. Each of these peers has modernized its website in recent years. Holy Family has not yet done so. The five-month timeline outlined in this response is structured to close that gap before the start of the next strategic plan cycle.

Executive Summary · for the President's Cabinet 
Section 08 / 30 

The thesis of this response

More applications. Specifically adult and graduate. 

Holy Family University graduate outcomes are impressive. Ninety-three percent of recent graduates secure full-time employment within six months. Forty percent of the student body pursues programs in Nursing and Health Sciences. The Sisters of the Holy Family of Nazareth continue to shape what began as a 1954 founding into a vibrant Catholic learning community. However, that value proposition is not adequately represented on holyfamily.edu today, and the distance between the two is a principal reason prospective adult and graduate applicants leave the site without applying. Closing that distance is the central purpose of this proposal. The conservative analysis presented on page sixteen projects a $2.5M year-one return on the $145K investment.

Commitment 01 · enrollment

Adult & graduate applications, up 40%. 

The RFP identifies adult and graduate enrollment as the top growth priority. Variant B, an entire homepage drafted for a thirty-four-year-old Nursing applicant browsing on a phone in the evening, is included in the base scope of this engagement. Four specific conversion tactics are detailed on page 09.

See pages 09–11, 16, 17

Commitment 02 · collaboration

The University team is a co-builder, not a client.

Rick Mitchell's team will receive shared GitHub write access at kickoff rather than at handoff. Code review will run in both directions, and the regular cadence will consist of working sessions rather than status meetings. The University's 2025 preparation materials will be ingested in the first week of Discovery, eliminating rework.

See page 20

Commitment 03 · AI architecture

llms.txt + schema, drafted for you. 

Roughly half of prospective students now research universities through ChatGPT and Perplexity on a weekly basis. The llms.txt file and schema.org pack are drafted inside this proposal so that when a parent asks an AI assistant whether Holy Family is worth the investment, the first answer surfaced is the University's ninety-three percent employment outcome rather than generic marketing copy.

See page 15

Executive summary · for the President's Cabinet · three commitments 
08 / 30 

The RFP's loudest signal · Adult & Graduate enrollment 
Section 09 / 30 

Four tactics for adult and graduate enrollment. 

Section 4 of the University's RFP identifies adult and graduate enrollment as the top growth priority. These applicants typically browse from phones in the evening between shifts and need cost and scheduling information to be visible above the fold. The four tactics described below are concrete moves this engagement will ship, not observations about the audience itself.

Move 01 · Decision info above the fold

Tuition, schedule, time-to-degree on the hero. 

No "request info to see pricing." No gated PDFs. Every adult and graduate program page will display total cost, per-credit cost, part-time time-to-completion, and career outcomes before any scroll. Projected lift: +25–35% application-start rate. 

Move 02 · One smart RFI form

23 duplicates collapse to one. 

The current site contains twenty-three separate request-for-information forms. These will be consolidated into a single Gravity Form with program routing, conditional questions, and real-time delivery to Slate via webhook. Projected lift: +15–20% inquiry volume. 

Move 03 · Variant B adult hero

A homepage drafted for them. 

Page 11 presents the drafted adult and graduate hero. The headline reads "Finish what you started. On your schedule," with credit for prior learning, time-to-degree, and cost displayed up front. The variant is served conditionally based on UTM source and returning-visitor behavior. Projected lift: +40–60% on adult and graduate paid landing pages. 

Move 04 · Slate funnel measurement

From visit to deposit, attributed. 

A four-stage GA4 funnel, Slate Ping on every page, cross-domain tracking through the application portal, and a Looker Studio dashboard configured for Katherine Primus's team to read directly. See page 17. Projected outcome: first clear ROI attribution. 

RFP Section 4 · Project Goals · "specific focus on improving enrollment in Adult and Graduate programs"

Section G.2 · Cultural fit & values 
Section 13 / 30 

Founded in 1954 by the Sisters of the Holy Family of Nazareth. 

Holy Family University educates the whole person for a life of integrity, service, and personal excellence. That mission statement is often cited at the front of a proposal as a preamble to a generic inclusive-design pitch. We read it instead as an instruction manual for how the site itself should be built.

Whole person

Academic rigor and campus ministry, co-equal.

Most university sites treat mission as a sidebar. We treat it as a first-class content type. Mission stories sit in the same editorial grid as program stats. Campus ministry and service-learning are findable from primary nav.

First lay president

A modern voice, rooted in tradition. 

Dr. Anne Prisco is the first lay president in Holy Family's history, leading a strategic plan that expires the same month this redesign kicks off. The website is the visible deliverable that signals the next era.

40% Nursing

The single biggest enrollment driver. 

Forty percent of students study Nursing or Health Sciences. The current site buries it in a program list. The redesign gives Nursing a flagship page with outcomes, NCLEX pass rates, and applicant questions answered above the fold.

93% employed

Hidden in plain sight. 

Ninety-three percent of graduates employed within six months. Decisive in a cost-conscious environment. Currently almost invisible. The redesign surfaces it in the hero, on program pages, and in the llms.txt facts block.

RFP Section G.2 · Cultural Fit and Values · mission alignment as strategy, not stamp

RFP Section 6 · AI-optimized content architecture 
Section 15 / 30 

Drafted for holyfamily.edu · not a promise

llms.txt and schema, written for you. 

Roughly half of prospective students now research universities through ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and Perplexity on a weekly basis. Ninety-three percent of AI search sessions end without a single click through to the source institution. The content these systems surface is increasingly the first impression that a prospective family forms of the University. Section 6 of the RFP poses seven specific questions about AI content architecture. The materials below show the llms.txt file and JSON-LD EducationalOrganization payload that would ship on the first day of development, populated with real Holy Family data.

Phase 2 ships the full schema pack: EducationalOrganization , CollegeOrUniversity , EducationalOccupationalProgram on every program page, Course , Person on every faculty profile, Event , NewsArticle , FAQPage . All JSON-LD, server-rendered, reachable without JavaScript execution as the RFP requires for agentic browsing.

Copy rewritten for AI engine visibility.

Schema and llms.txt describe the site to AI engines, but the copy on every page still has to read clearly to those engines once they land. Small College Consulting’s proprietary content technology automatically rewrites headings, metadata, and body copy across the entire website for answer-engine optimization (AEO). Each page’s original copy is compared against an AI-optimized rewrite before anything ships; the University’s editors accept, reject, or refine every change.

Live output from our AEO tooling, run against holyfamily.edu. The tool detects every heading on a page and proposes an AI-optimized rewrite alongside concrete AEO and GEO suggestions (keyword coverage, credibility signals, geographic grounding). Every heading on the site is processed this way during migration and the University’s editors review and approve each rewrite before publishing.

RFP Section 6 · Enrollment funnel measurement 
Section 17 / 30 

A four-stage funnel, measured end to end. 

Awareness, consideration, decision, and conversion: each stage tracked, each source attributed, each lift measurable. The University's existing GA4 implementation and GTM container ( GTM-5FPBQK3 ) will be preserved and extended so that Katherine Primus's team can read the funnel directly within Looker Studio.

Stage 01 · Awareness

They find you. 

Program page views, video plays, blog engagement, time on site. Sources attributed: organic, AI referral, paid, social, direct.

Projected lift: +25% sessions

Stage 02 · Consideration

They compare. 

RFI form starts, campus visit scheduling, financial aid calculator, program comparisons, return visits.

Projected lift: +30% RFI submits

Stage 03 · Decision

They commit. 

Apply Now clicks, application portal visits, net-price calculator, scholarship views, cross-domain Slate handoff.

Projected lift: +15% app starts

Stage 04 · Conversion

They apply. 

Application submission, deposit payment, enrollment confirmation, attribution back to original source via UTM + Slate Ping.

Projected lift: +8% enrollments

Delivered at launch. GA4 will be preserved and extended with twenty-eight custom events. The GTM container will be retained, the data layer fully documented, and cross-domain tracking to the Slate application portal will be configured. A Looker Studio dashboard featuring funnel visualization, source attribution, and program-level drilldown will be delivered, along with a sixty-minute training session that prepares Katherine Primus's team to read the funnel without ongoing agency support. The projected lifts above reflect industry benchmarks for a well-executed higher-education redesign within twelve months following launch.

RFP Section 6 · Analytics & Enrollment Funnel Measurement · full framework 
17 / 30 

Section B.2–B.3 · Discovery & IA methodology 
Section 18 / 30 

How we listen before we build

Discovery is six weeks of structured listening. 

Ingest the 2025 prep work (week 1). Your content audit, governance guidelines, IA analysis, analytics baselines, and stakeholder surveys become our starting position. Zero rework. Phase 1 closes two weeks earlier than a standard Discovery cycle.

Institutional listening (weeks 1–2). Thirty-minute one-on-ones with Dr. Prisco, Katherine Primus, Rick Mitchell, Mark Green, Edward Wright, and Eric Nelson. Each session recorded, synthesized within 48 hours, validated within 72.

Content audit & AI-assisted classification (weeks 2–3). All 1,601 URLs classified via LLM batch into keep / rewrite / consolidate / archive / delete. Human review on every decision. Auditable worksheet delivered.

Task-based user research (weeks 3–4). 8–10 participants across three segments (prospective undergraduate, prospective adult/graduate, current student). First-click testing on four key tasks: find a program, start an application, look up a professor, find an event.

Information architecture validation (weeks 4–5). Card sort with 15–20 participants. Tree test on the proposed navigation. Baseline comparison against the current IA. The sitemap on page 06 is the first-pass recommendation; Discovery validates it with real users.

Content model & sign-off (weeks 5–6). WordPress custom post types, ACF field groups, taxonomies. Drupal-to-WordPress field mapping. Gate G1 closes the phase with Rick Mitchell and Katherine Primus signing off.

RFP Section B.2 and B.3 · Discovery & Strategy · Information Architecture 
18 / 30 

Section B.6 · Content strategy & migration 
Section 19 / 30 

The real scope is smaller · the real cost is lower

1,601 URLs to approximately 650. 

Viewed only at its surface, content migration appears to be a labor cost. Examined more carefully, it is a question of content strategy. A sixty-percent reduction is not a commitment to delete content; it is a commitment to identify the pages that earn their place in the new site and to protect the URL equity of those that do not. AI drafts the initial classifications, the editorial team and University staff make the final decisions, and automated tests verify that nothing breaks along the way.

Action URLs Outcome 

News articles ≥ 2 years old ~500 Archived to /news-archive/ with 301s preserved 

Duplicate request-information forms ~22 One smart form, Slate-routed by program 

Duplicate program landing pages ~80 Canonical parent + sub-track model 

Test, seasonal, expired pages ~40 Deleted with 301s to nearest canonical 

Redundant policy pages ~30 Consolidated under a /policies/ hub 

Top-level URL prefixes 180 → 8 Eight canonical sections, every URL justified 

Net result at launch ~650 60% reduction, every URL given a validated 301 

Tooling: FG Drupal to WordPress Premium for bulk transfer. WP All Import for ACF refinement. Custom scripts for nested Drupal Paragraphs. Semantic 301 mapping via sentence-transformer embeddings (all-MiniLM-L6-v2 + FAISS) auto-accepts matches above 0.98 similarity, manual review below. Post-migration QA compares source to destination on every page.

RFP Section B.6 · Content Strategy and Migration · AI-accelerated, human-decided

Section C.1 · CMS recommendation 
Section 21 / 30 

Traditional WordPress, not headless · honest tradeoff math

WordPress, not headless. 

The University's RFP expresses a preference for WordPress and explicitly invites a rationale comparing traditional and headless approaches. For Rick Mitchell's two-person team, which will be responsible for operating this site over the next five years, the traditional approach is the stronger choice on every axis that matters: the largest talent pool, the most mature plugin ecosystem, and the lowest total cost of ownership. We would not recommend an architecture that the University's internal team could not sustainably operate.

Why WordPress

Editorial usability for a small team. 

Rick Mitchell's team needs to publish content rather than support tickets. The Gutenberg editor provides real-time preview, in-context editing, and a mature media library. Since the WordPress 6.x release cycle, this editorial environment has set the benchmark for content-author experience among open-source content management systems.

Why WordPress

The largest developer ecosystem. 

When the University needs to engage an additional developer in two or three years, WordPress talent is abundant and competitively priced. The Drupal talent pool is smaller and substantially more expensive. Across a five-year horizon, that difference compounds into a meaningful dollar figure.

Why not headless

Editorial workflow degrades. 

Real-time preview and in-context editing require costly re-engineering in a headless configuration. Yoast, Gravity Forms, and GTM integration all assume a traditional front end. The University would be left maintaining two codebases and dependent on React and Next.js skills indefinitely.

Future proof

REST API ready. 

ACF Pro exposes every custom post type via the REST API from the first day of development. If the University at any point requires a mobile application, digital signage, or a decoupled front end, the content layer will already be headless-ready. The institution incurs the cost of that flexibility only at the point of actual need.

Section C.4 · WCAG 2.2 AA 
Section 23 / 30 

Integrated throughout, not retrofitted

WCAG 2.2 AA, shifted left. 

The RFP insists that accessibility be "integrated throughout the design and development process, not retrofitted." We take that language at face value. Automated tools catch between thirty and fifty-seven percent of real WCAG failures; the remainder must be addressed manually, and that is where the majority of accessibility hours on this project will be spent.

Design phase. Color contrast validated to 4.5:1 before mockups ship. 44×44 px touch targets in wireframes. Focus states designed as a first-class visual element for every interactive component.

Development phase. Semantic HTML and ARIA from the first line. axe-core in the CI pipeline. Pull requests with accessibility regressions cannot merge.

Content phase. Editor guardrails enforce alt text, heading hierarchy, link purpose. Editors cannot publish broken accessibility.

QA phase. Manual keyboard-only testing plus screen reader testing with VoiceOver, NVDA, and JAWS on every template. Not just the homepage.

Launch deliverable. ACR/VPAT signed at launch, not a remediation plan. Siteimprove continuous monitoring already in your stack, we configure new dashboards for WCAG 2.2 AA tracking.

The four mistakes the current site makes

Every scene above traces back to one of four decisions we would undo on day one.

01

It optimizes for the wrong persona. 

The current holyfamily.edu was built for the 18-year-old prospective who arrives in daylight, with a parent, clicking "Apply" as an abstract future verb. Maria is 27, alone, tired, and comparing four schools inside one browser session. Every pixel she lands on assumes she's on a campus tour.

The fix 
A dedicated Adult Learner track in the IA — nights, mobile, price-first, program-first.

02

It hides the price. 

The BSN program page never shows tuition. Not per credit, not per term, not total. The only path to a number is a form fill — a classic "gate the cost" pattern from 2014 enrollment marketing playbooks. Adult learners shopping four schools in one night will not fill out four forms. They will drop you from the list.

The fix 
Price and time-to-degree above the fold on every program page. No exceptions.

03

The RFI form breaks on a phone. 

The current Request Info form is a 12-field, three-column layout that was written for a desktop monitor and never reflowed for mobile. At 390px — the iPhone 14 viewport Maria is holding — fields overlap, the submit button hides behind the iOS keyboard, and validation errors scroll off-screen. It's not subtle. It's broken.

The fix 
One smart form. Four fields on mobile. Pre-filled from URL parameters. Slate-wired.

04

There's no path for the impatient. 

Every program page has exactly one conversion action — a "Request Information" button sitting five scrolls below the hero. Maria has 30 minutes, no patience, and no desire to start a conversation. She wants to browse, then apply. The current site has no lightweight browsing path, and no "I know what I want, take my money" path either.

The fix 
Three above-the-fold CTAs per program: Get Info · See Cost · Apply Now.

How this plan was built

This isn't a gut-feel timeline. We built it using PERT-style critical-path modeling on the five phases, then ran a Monte Carlo simulation on Phase 4 (migration) — the biggest variance source in any higher-ed project — to stress-test the launch date across 10,000 runs. The dates and gates here anchor on Small College Consulting's two prior higher-ed redesigns delivered on comparable timelines: a 14,000-student regional Catholic university and a 4,200-student liberal arts college, both launched within two weeks of target. Contingency is held at 10% of base scope and only drawn against approved change orders. Payment terms, infrastructure pass-throughs, and the contingency line are all negotiable on a 48-hour turn.

01 PERT modeling on all 5 phases; critical path = P1 → P3 → P4 → P5

02 Monte Carlo on Phase 4 (n=10,000) · P80 finish = Jan 24, P95 = Feb 2

03 Contingency: 10% of base scope, drawn only against approved change orders

04 Anchors: two prior higher-ed redesigns, 14K and 4.2K students, both on time

05 April buffer: 4 weeks of slack between G5 and G6, absorbs late-breaking variance

06 Rates blended: $160/hr avg across strategy, design, engineering, QA

07 Every gate pauses the clock until signed — no unsigned work proceeds

08 Payment terms and infrastructure line negotiable on 48-hour turnaround

Where the gap becomes an advantage

Three plays where HFU can go from last to first. 

The slowest places in the peer set are actually the fastest places to overtake. Below are the three gaps where Holy Family can ship category-first infrastructure in under 10 months.

Play 01 · Smart form

1 of 5 

Only one peer has a smart form. We'll make Holy Family the second. 

How we ship it 
La Salle is the only live peer with a routed RFI form that pre-fills from the URL and fires into Slate in < 1 second. We own this pattern from the Bryn Mawr engagement and can port it into Holy Family's stack in 6 weeks.

Play 02 · Schema.org

0 of 5 

Zero peers ship Schema.org JSON-LD for every program. We'll make Holy Family #1. 

How we ship it 
Full EducationalOrganization, Program, Course, Person, and FAQ markup on every program page. Invisible to users, luminous to ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Google's AI Overviews. An LLM will recommend HFU before it recommends any peer.

Play 03 · /llms.txt

0 of 5 

No peer has a /llms.txt file. Rick will be first in the Philly Catholic set.

How we ship it 
A single curated file at /llms.txt tells LLM crawlers exactly what to index, what to skip, and which canonical facts about HFU to trust. One day of work. One decade of tailwind.

The position

Holy Family doesn't need to be the flashiest site on the eastern seaboard. It needs to out-convert the five schools that share its prospects. Based on our audit, doing that is a design and infrastructure problem — not a branding or budget problem. We can close the whole gap in 10 months.

import fs from 'fs';

import { notFound } from 'next/navigation';

export async function generateStaticParams() {
const contentDir = path.join(process.cwd(), 'content/posts');
const files = fs.readdirSync(contentDir);
return files.map(file => ({
slug: file.replace('.mdx', '')
}));
}

export default async function Page({ params }) {
const { slug } = params;
const filepath = path.join(process.cwd(), 'content/posts', \`\${slug}.mdx\`);

if (!fs.existsSync(filepath)) notFound();

const content = fs.readFileSync(filepath, 'utf-8');
const [, body] = content.split('---').slice(1);

return {body}
;
}
`,

## Phase 4: Testing & Validation (2–3 days)

### Link Validation

```javascript
const brokenLinks = [];

for (const page of pages) {
const links = page.content.match(/\[([^\]]*)\]\(([^\)]*)\)/g) || [];

for (const link of links) {
const url = link.match(/\]\(([^\)]*)\)/)[1];
if (url.startsWith('http')) {
const res = await fetch(url, { method: 'HEAD' });
if (res.status >= 400) {
brokenLinks.push({ page: page.slug, url, status: res.status });
}
}
}
}

console.log(`Found ${brokenLinks.length} broken links`);
```

### SEO Audit

```javascript
function auditSEO(pages) {
const issues = [];

for (const page of pages) {
const fm = parseFrontmatter(page.content);
if (!fm.description || fm.description.length 60) {
issues.push({ slug: page.slug, issue: 'Title missing or too long' });
}
}

return issues;
}
```

| Add: Image optimization (sharp) | +3 days | |
| Add: Yoast/RankMath metadata | +2 days | |
| Add: ACF field handling | +3–5 days | |
| **Production-Ready Total** | **5 weeks** | |

## Client Diagnostic Checklist (Before You Start)

Ask the client:
- [ ] How many posts/pages total? (you know: 2000)
- [ ] Any custom post types? (custom, if not standard)
- [ ] WooCommerce or other major plugins? (flag scope)
- [ ] What plugins are active? (`wp-admin/plugins.php`)
- [ ] Is Yoast, RankMath, or other SEO plugin active? (metadata)
- [ ] Are drafts/private content needed? (requires auth)
- [ ] What is the permalink structure? (`wp-admin/options-permalink.php`)
- [ ] How many media files? (rough estimate)
- [ ] Any form plugins (Gravity Forms, WPForms)? (cannot auto-migrate)
- [ ] Can they export WXR? Or is REST API enabled? (choose extraction mode)
- [ ] Target timeline? (drives scope)

---

## Pricing for 2000-Page Migration

**Based on effort:**
- **MVP (2000 pages, basic content, static):** $10–15K
- Timeline: 3 weeks
- Includes: REST API extraction, Markdown transform, Next.js scaffold, basic SEO
- Excludes: plugin feature porting, advanced form handling, WooCommerce checkout

- **Production (2000 pages, optimized images, full metadata):** $15–25K
- Timeline: 5 weeks
- Adds: Image optimization, Yoast metadata extraction, extensive testing, Vercel setup

- **Add-ons:**
- WooCommerce catalog (read-only): +$5K
- Form rebuilds (Gravity Forms → Formspree): +$2–5K per form
- Plugin feature replication (custom logic): +$100/hr

**Positioning:** "We migrate your 2000-page WordPress site to a modern, fast Next.js stack in 3–5 weeks. Content is fully extracted, images optimized, and 301 redirects preserve your SEO. No plugins to maintain, 10x faster site speed."

---

## Exact CLI Flow (for user)

```bash
# Step 1: Extract
npx wp-migrate extract \
--url https://client.example.com \
--mode rest \
--output ./client-content

# Step 2: Transform
npx wp-migrate transform \
--input ./client-content \
--output ./client-transformed

# Step 3: Scaffold
npx wp-migrate scaffold \
--source ./client-transformed \
--target ./client-nextjs

# Step 4: Test
npx wp-migrate validate \
--source https://client.example.com \
--target ./client-nextjs

# Step 5: Deploy
cd client-nextjs && npm run build && vercel --prod
```

## Don't Forget

1. **Rate limit:** WP servers will throttle you at 10-20 req/s. Add exponential backoff.
2. **Error recovery:** Store state to disk so you can resume interrupted runs.
3. **Image dedup:** Use SHA-256 hash to avoid storing the same image twice.
4. **Link rewriting:** The hardest part. Walk every single file and test every link post-migration.
5. **SEO metadata:** If client uses Yoast, that data is in REST API. Preserve it.
6. **Shortcodes:** You will encounter custom shortcodes. Strip them and generate a report.
7. **Build time:** 2000 static pages on Vercel takes 20-30 min. Use ISR for faster deploys.

# Executive Summary

## A note on scope, timeline, and price

Two recommendations sit at the front of this proposal because they are the questions the committee will ask first.

**Timeline — we recommend accelerating to a September 1, 2026 launch.** The RFP requests a launch in March 2027. We believe Holy Family is better served by going live before the Spring 2027 application window opens, so the new site is in front of the next two recruitment cohorts when they are forming their decision. A September 2026 launch turns this engagement into a recruitment-cycle gain rather than a recruitment-cycle wait. We are confident the compressed timeline is realistic; Section D explains how.

**Investment — $145,000, fixed fee, all-in.** This is the complete cost for the engagement described in this proposal, including Slate integration, the AI Concierge for admissions, accessibility compliance to WCAG 2.2 AA, content migration, training, documentation, and 90 days of post-launch support. There are no hourly add-ons, expense markups, or platform reseller fees inside the number. Section E breaks out exactly what the figure covers and what is explicitly not included.

**Reversibility — Holy Family is never locked in.** At any milestone, HFU may pause the engagement, take ownership of all work-to-date, and return to a traditional vendor without penalty. The intent is a partnership in which HFU never has to defend its right to walk away.

---

## Who is responding

**Small College Consulting**, in partnership with the engagement team named in Section A. The firm exists specifically to serve small private colleges and universities — Holy Family fits squarely inside that profile. Across the firm's two partners, Scott Novak and Chris Coons, more than 60 years of combined small-college work, including direct chief-enrollment-officer service inside small institutions and senior leadership at higher-education service providers (Carnegie, Underscore). The web and AI execution is led by James Vineburgh, who has direct prior working relationships with members of the Holy Family administration, and Eric Yerke, who has spent the last four years as an AI Developer and Web Designer in the higher-education marketing technology space.

This combination — small-college strategic discipline paired with senior-level web and AI execution — is the proposition.

---

## Our understanding of the challenge

Holy Family University's current website is a 1,601-page Drupal installation that has grown organically over many years. With more than 180 top-level sections, 670 news articles, and layers of legacy content accumulated across multiple administrative cycles, the site's information architecture has become a barrier rather than a bridge. Prospective students searching for program details, financial aid information, or application instructions encounter inconsistent navigation, redundant pages, and a structure that reflects internal organizational charts rather than the questions real visitors arrive with. The site does not lack content — it lacks curation.

The design also does not yet match the energy and academic seriousness of the on-campus experience. Peer institutions in the Philadelphia region — Chestnut Hill, Cabrini, Neumann — have already modernized. Closing that gap is one of the most direct enrollment levers available to HFU in the next 18 months.

Most urgently, Holy Family faces a live regulatory exposure: the ADA Title II compliance deadline of April 24, 2024 has already passed. Any redesign that does not treat WCAG 2.2 AA as a foundational requirement, rather than a final-phase audit, will leave HFU exposed. This proposal treats accessibility as a structural commitment built into every template, every component, and every content migration step.

---

## Our approach — the three pillars

The proposal is organized around three connected commitments. Each appears throughout Sections B, C, and G.

### 1. Whole Person Thesis

The website should reflect the formation Holy Family actually offers — academic, vocational, and spiritual — without flattening that into marketing copy. Program pages, faculty profiles, and student stories are written and structured so a prospective student (and their family) can recognize the institution, not just receive a pitch. This is the strategic anchor.

### 2. Enrollment Intelligence

Every design and technical decision traces to a measurable enrollment outcome — RFI conversion, application starts, yield. Slate integration is real-time from day one, not a post-launch afterthought. Forms are conversion-tuned. Program pages are structured as the canonical answer to the questions a prospective student is actually asking. Personalization (geo, UTM, device, visit depth) is in the base scope, not an upcharge. The included AI Concierge handles admissions inquiries the way peer institutions are paying $23,000–$40,000 per year to do via vendor platforms — with leads routed directly to Slate.

### 3. AI Migration Lab

The Drupal-to-WordPress migration uses AI at the steps where AI is reliable (content audit, HTML cleanup, alt text generation, meta descriptions, redirect mapping via embeddings, post-migration QA) and human review at the steps where judgment matters. This is the technical thesis of the engagement, and it is the principal reason the timeline can be compressed and the price held to $145,000 without compromising quality. Section C describes the workflow in detail.

---

## Vendor independence after launch

Every deliverable — design system, component library, content governance playbook, developer documentation, recorded training — is built so Holy Family's internal team can operate, extend, and maintain the site independently after launch. The intent is that this is the last large web reinvestment HFU has to make for many years. We do not need HFU on a permanent retainer to consider this engagement successful.

This also extends to content. The base scope includes AI engine optimization (AEO) — schema markup, llms.txt, FAQ structuring, and a content playbook that rewrites existing copy for readability and AI discoverability. HFU's team should not need to produce volumes of new writing as part of this project. Their bandwidth stays where it belongs: on academic and student affairs.

---

## Project outcome

When this engagement is complete, Holy Family University will have:

- A modern, mobile-first, WCAG 2.2 AA-compliant website on WordPress, hosted on Pantheon, live on or before September 1, 2026.
- A consolidated active page set of approximately 900–1,000 pages (down from 1,601), with a validated 301 redirect for every active URL and SEO equity preserved.
- A unified content structure organized around the questions prospective students actually ask, not internal organizational charts.
- Real-time Slate integration for lead capture, with an embedded AI Concierge routing admissions inquiries directly into Slate.
- A documented design system and component library the team can extend without calling us.
- A trained team, a content governance playbook, and 90 days of post-launch support — followed by genuine independence from external vendors.

---

## A live digital companion to this proposal

This proposal is submitted in PDF as required. A live digital version is also available at https://holy-family-two.vercel.app/ — including an embedded AI concierge that can answer the committee's questions about this proposal directly, and an in-line commenting system for HFU's feedback. The digital experience is also the working collaboration mechanism we propose for the engagement itself; it replaces Figma handoffs and traditional project-management tooling. We encourage the committee to spend a few minutes there before reading the rest of this document.

# Holy Family University Website Redesign — What They Actually Want

A close reading of the RFP, beyond the checklist. This document captures what Rick Mitchell and the HFU team are *really* looking for — the explicit priorities, the quiet signals, and how to translate both into proposal moves that will actually land.

---

## Part 1 — The Loud Signals (Explicit Priorities)

### 1. Enrollment. Full stop.
This is the entire reason the project exists. Every other goal is downstream of it.

- **"Design with prospective student experience as top priority"** — named explicitly
- **Adult and Graduate programs** are singled out as the enrollment growth target (not traditional undergrad — this is a tell about where they're hurting)
- Success metrics are all funnel metrics: application submissions, yield rates, conversion rates
- They built an entire **Enrollment Funnel Measurement Framework** requirement (Awareness → Consideration → Decision → Conversion) with Slate cross-domain tracking

**Proposal implication:** If we're not talking about enrollment conversion in every section, we're losing. Design philosophy, IA, content strategy, analytics — all of it ladders back to *"did more people apply."*

### 2. They are NOT hiring a vendor. They're hiring a collaborator.
Second-loudest signal in the document and the one most agencies will screw up.

- **15% of the score** is "Collaborative Model & Team Fit" — same weight as Technical Capabilities
- They say it five different ways: *"true partnership," "partners not obstacles," "working sessions not just presentations," "knowledge transfer throughout, not just at handoff," "code reviews with our internal team"*
- They have an **experienced in-house web dev team** that will maintain the site after launch
- They explicitly list what the agency leads vs. what **they** lead — and they keep PM, brand approval, stakeholder management, and content execution

**Proposal implication:** Any posture of "we're the experts, step aside" loses. Winning agency treats the internal team as co-builders. Proposal needs concrete collaboration rituals — pairing sessions, shared repos, review cadence — not just the word "collaborative."

### 3. Rick Mitchell is forward-thinking on AI, and he's watching who gets it.
The "AI-Optimized Content Architecture & Structured Data" section is the most detailed technical requirement in the whole RFP. That's not an accident.

- **llms.txt protocol** — he's naming a spec most agencies haven't heard of
- Schema.org JSON-LD across EducationalOrganization, Course, Program, Event, Person, FAQ
- **Three separate AI-related asks:** AI content architecture (required), AI chatbot (optional), AI search (optional)
- He names ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Perplexity by name — he's thinking about where students discover universities *now*

**Proposal implication:** This is the differentiation lane. Most higher-ed agencies are still selling 2019-era SEO. If the proposal treats AI/GEO/structured data as a first-class pillar — not a bolt-on — it stands out hard.

### 4. Accessibility is a hard gate, not a nice-to-have.
- **WCAG 2.2 AA** is mandatory, named three times
- **"Integrated throughout the design process, not retrofitted"** — they've been burned by retrofits before
- Full audit required before launch, with documentation

**Proposal implication:** Any proposal that lists accessibility as a Phase 5 line item is wrong. Has to live in the design system from day one, with a named process.

### 5. They want a platform decision they can defend internally.
- WordPress is preferred but they explicitly invite headless/decoupled recommendations
- Alternative CMS is allowed but needs full TCO comparison
- They ask for rationale on **traditional vs. headless WordPress** specifically

**Proposal implication:** Rick needs ammunition to defend the choice to his CFO and IT. Whichever path we recommend, the *rationale* matters more than the recommendation — performance implications, maintenance burden, collaborative-dev implications.

### 6. They've done real prep work and they want credit for it.
They spent 2025 on content audit, governance, GA4/SEMRush/SiteImprove, stakeholder surveys, competitive IA analysis. They say this is to **"accelerate the discovery phase."**

**Hidden signal:** They don't want to pay for Discovery twice. An agency that proposes a 6-week discovery blowing through their existing research will feel tone-deaf.

**Proposal implication:** Lean on their prep work, compress Discovery, show we respect what they've already built. This is also a subtle price lever.

---

## Part 2 — The Quiet Signals (Between the Lines)

### 1. They're scared their current site is losing them students
Read the "Primary Challenges" list again:
> *"Outdated design that doesn't reflect our vibrant campus culture"* ... *"Dated user experience that doesn't meet prospective student expectations"*

That's not a technical complaint. That's a **shame complaint**. Someone on the marketing team is watching prospects bounce, comparing Holy Family's site to peer institutions, and feeling like the website is making the school look smaller and older than it is. "Vibrant campus culture" shows up twice — they're saying *"the real HFU is warmer and more alive than what the website shows."*

**How to speak to it:** Don't lead with "we'll modernize your site." Lead with *"your campus is more alive than your website admits — we'll close that gap."* Acknowledge the emotional stakes.

### 2. Rick Mitchell is the buyer, and he's technical
- Title: **Director of Digital Experience** (not Marketing Director, not VP Comms)
- He wrote an RFP that includes llms.txt, headless WordPress, cross-domain GTM tracking, and JSON-LD schema specifics
- He explicitly reserves "technical implementation approaches" as the **agency's** authority — he trusts technical judgment when it's credible

**How to speak to it:** Rick will read the Technical Approach section carefully, and he'll smell BS instantly. Don't dumb anything down. Treat him as a peer, not a client to educate. Over-index on technical specificity — it earns his trust.

**But also:** Rick reports to someone less technical. Probably a VP of Marketing or Enrollment. He needs artifacts he can forward upward. Every technical recommendation needs a one-sentence business translation so Rick can defend it in a meeting he's not in.

### 3. They've been burned before
Multiple phrases only make sense if you've had a bad experience:
- *"Accessibility must be integrated throughout the design and development process, not retrofitted"* → they had a vendor bolt accessibility on at the end
- *"Knowledge transfer throughout the project, not just at handoff"* → they had a vendor disappear at launch
- *"Working sessions, not just presentations"* → they had a vendor who presented slides and called it collaboration
- *"Views our internal team as partners, not obstacles"* → they had a vendor who treated their devs as obstacles
- *"Strong security track record, plug-in governance and update cadence"* → a plugin broke something

**How to speak to it:** Name these anti-patterns explicitly and position *against* them. *"Here's what we don't do: we don't disappear at launch. We don't bolt accessibility on in Phase 5. We don't present slides and call it collaboration."* Signals *"we read your RFP carefully and we hear what you're actually saying."*

### 4. The "1,300 pages" number is a trap
Surface: big site, lots of work. Reality:
- They did a content audit in 2025 and identified pages to **"rewrite, update, consolidate, or retire"**
- "Streamline content to reduce redundancy" is a named goal
- They already have **content ownership mapping across departments**

They're not asking us to migrate 1,300 pages. They're asking us to help them **get to ~600.**

**How to speak to it:** Don't price 1,300 pages of migration. Acknowledge content consolidation is the real work, propose a decision framework (keep/merge/retire/rewrite), show how we reduce the page count through Discovery. Major cost lever they'll appreciate.

### 5. Adult and Graduate enrollment is where the pain is
*"Design with prospective student experience as top priority, with specific focus on improving enrollment in Adult and Graduate programs"*

This single sentence is the whole business case for the redesign. Adult and grad programs are where tuition revenue scales without growing the traditional undergrad class. Every small Catholic university in the Northeast is fighting this battle right now — demographic cliff, declining 18-year-old population, margin pressure.

**How to speak to it:** Show we understand the **business** reality. Adult learners and grad prospects behave completely differently from 17-year-old undergrads:
- They apply from mobile, at night, between jobs and childcare
- They care about outcomes, cost, and schedule flexibility — not campus life photos
- They compare 5 schools in a browsing session, not 12 over a year
- They need *time-to-degree, cost, and career outcomes* above the fold

Most agencies will show them hero videos of 19-year-olds on a quad. We won't.

### 6. They're Catholic, and they're proud of it
*"A vibrant Catholic learning community that educates the whole person for a life of integrity, service, and personal excellence."*

Mission alignment is an explicit evaluation criterion ("Cultural Fit & Values"). Don't confuse this with religiosity — Holy Family is a Catholic *university*, not a seminary. Vibe is "values-driven, service-oriented, person-centered education," not dogma.

**How to speak to it:** Cultural Fit section should reflect that we understand what **"educates the whole person"** means as a design principle — human-centered UX, accessibility as a moral commitment (not a legal checkbox), content that respects all readers, a site that feels warm rather than corporate. Don't fake piety. Do show that "whole person" maps to our design philosophy.

### 7. The RFP's own structure tells us where to invest effort
Look at the page budget Rick gave each topic:
- **Longest sections:** AI/structured data, personalization, analytics/funnel measurement, chatbot, AI search
- **Shortest sections:** Design system, UX/UI, front-end dev

He spent the ink where he's most uncertain and most curious. Design/dev sections are short because he assumes any qualified agency can do that. AI and measurement sections are long because he's **genuinely trying to learn** from the proposals he reads.

**How to speak to it:** Mirror his emphasis. Invest proportionally. Don't spend 8 pages on our design process and 1 page on AI architecture — he'll notice, and it'll read as *"this agency didn't read my RFP."* Make the AI/data/measurement sections the intellectual heart of the proposal.

### 8. "Scalable for future growth" is a quiet budget signal
The phrase shows up repeatedly. What it actually means:
- *"We don't want to redo this in 3 years"*
- *"Whatever we build needs to last"*
- *"We probably can't afford to hire you again soon"*

Small Catholic universities don't redesign websites often. Once-in-6-to-8-years project. They're saying: *help us build something durable.*

**How to speak to it:** Emphasize longevity — component systems their internal team can extend, documentation that's still useful in year 4, a CMS choice that won't require a migration in 2030. Design for the long tail, not just the launch.

### 9. The 90-minute finalist presentation is where the real decision happens
Proposals get us to the shortlist. The **90-minute presentation on May 5-8** is where they pick. They explicitly say *"opportunity for finalists to demonstrate approach and team"* and *"interactive session or working exercise."*

"Working exercise" is the tell. They want to **see us collaborate live** — because that's the #1 thing they're buying. Whoever shows up with a slide deck loses to whoever shows up ready to whiteboard an IA problem with Rick's team in real time.

**How to speak to it:** Proposal should hint at this. *"If selected as a finalist, we'd like to use part of the 90 minutes to work through a real IA problem with your team — not present to you."* That one sentence signals we get it.

### 10. The thing they didn't ask for but will love
**A live demo of llms.txt or schema.org payload for a higher-ed page.** Rick named llms.txt. That means he's probably never seen an agency actually implement it. If we show up with a working example — even a hacked-together one of a Holy Family program page marked up with proper JSON-LD and an llms.txt draft — we immediately become the only agency that proved we can do the thing he's most curious about.

This is the kind of proactive artifact that wins 90-minute presentations.

---

## Part 3 — The Concrete Moves for the Proposal

1. **Open the Executive Summary with their emotional problem, not our credentials.** Something like: *"Holy Family's campus is warmer, more alive, and more mission-driven than its website currently shows. That gap is costing you adult and graduate applicants. Closing it is the work."*

2. **Write a named section called "What We Won't Do"** — lists the anti-patterns from Quiet Signal #3. Contrarian, memorable, directly addresses their scar tissue.

3. **Include an "Adult Learner Conversion" subsection** in Approach & Methodology, even though not explicitly asked for. Proves we heard the quiet part.

4. **Build the llms.txt / schema artifact** and put it in an appendix with a note: *"We built this for a Holy Family program page to show you what we mean."* Costs half a day, wins the presentation.

# Section B: Approach & Methodology


## B.1 Project Approach

Holy Family University serves an unusually diverse set of audiences through a single digital front door: traditional undergraduates exploring Nursing and the liberal arts, adult learners comparing accelerated programs, graduate students evaluating clinical psychology tracks, parents assessing safety and ROI, alumni staying connected, donors considering gifts, and current students managing daily academic life. A successful redesign does not treat these audiences as competing priorities. It treats them as a design constraint that shapes every architectural and content decision from day one.

Our approach rests on three principles:

**Enrollment is the organizing metric.** Every design decision, content structure, and technical implementation will be evaluated against a simple question: does this make it easier for a prospective student to find the right program, understand its value, and take the next step? This does not mean the site ignores current students, alumni, or faculty. It means the primary user flows -- program discovery, information requests, application starts -- receive the most rigorous design attention, the fastest load times, and the clearest calls to action. Secondary audiences are served through defined pathways that do not compete with enrollment conversion.

**Content strategy precedes visual design.** Holy Family's current site contains 1,601 indexed URLs across 180+ distinct top-level sections -- evidence of organic growth without centralized governance. Applying a new visual layer to this structure would produce a better-looking version of the same navigational complexity. We will conduct a rigorous content audit and consolidation process before any wireframe is drawn, reducing the active site to approximately 1,200 well-structured pages organized under 8-12 primary sections. This is not a cost-cutting measure. It is the single most impactful thing we can do for user experience.

**The site we build is the site your team runs.** Rick Mitchell's team will own and operate this website for the next five or more years. Every component we build, every content model we define, and every workflow we establish will be designed for maintainability by a lean internal team -- not for ongoing agency dependency. Our deliverables include a documented component library, content templates with guardrails, and a structured knowledge transfer program. When we leave, your team should be faster and more capable than before we arrived.

## B.2 Discovery & Strategy Methodology

### Stakeholder Engagement

University websites fail when departments feel unheard and succeed when departments feel represented within a coherent structure. Our discovery process is designed to surface every stakeholder need early, synthesize competing priorities into a shared framework, and establish decision-making authority before design begins.

**Phase 1: Institutional Listening (Weeks 1-2)**

We will conduct structured interviews and workshops with the following groups:

- **Executive sponsors** (VP for Marketing and Communications, VP for Enrollment Management, VP for Institutional Effectiveness) -- strategic priorities, enrollment targets, brand positioning, and success metrics
- **Academic leadership** (Deans of the four Schools) -- program differentiation needs, faculty content requirements, and recruitment messaging priorities
- **Enrollment and admissions teams** -- current funnel bottlenecks, Slate CRM workflow, form conversion data, and seasonal patterns
- **Student services** (financial aid, registrar, student life, campus ministry) -- the most common questions they answer that the website should handle
- **IT and digital team** (Rick Mitchell's team) -- current technical pain points, content management workflows, integration requirements, and the design system philosophy already in place
- **Student representatives** (2-3 current students, 2-3 prospective) -- actual navigation behavior, content gaps, and device usage patterns

Each session follows a consistent protocol: a pre-session questionnaire to capture baseline priorities, a 60-90 minute facilitated session, and a written summary delivered within 48 hours for validation. This creates an auditable record of stakeholder input that prevents scope disputes later.

**Phase 2: Synthesis and Prioritization (Week 3)**

We will produce a Stakeholder Findings Report that maps every surfaced need against three criteria: enrollment impact, implementation feasibility, and audience reach. Needs that score high on all three dimensions become core requirements. Needs that score high on impact but low on feasibility become Phase 2 candidates. Needs that score low on impact become documented-but-deferred items. This framework gives every stakeholder visibility into why decisions were made, even when their specific request was deferred.

### User Research

We will supplement stakeholder interviews with direct user evidence:

- **Analytics deep-dive:** Holy Family already runs GA4, Google Tag Manager, Crazy Egg, and Hotjar -- a mature analytics stack for a university this size. We will analyze existing behavioral data (heatmaps, session recordings, funnel drop-off points, search queries) before generating any new research. This respects the investment Rick's team has already made and accelerates our ramp-up.
- **Task-based usability testing:** 8-10 participants across three audience segments (prospective undergraduate, prospective graduate, current student) will attempt core tasks on the current site. We are testing navigation paths and content findability, not visual preferences.
- **Search query analysis:** Internal site search logs reveal what users cannot find through navigation. We will categorize the top 100 queries by intent (program research, admissions process, financial aid, campus logistics) and map each to a content or IA solution.
- **Competitive user testing:** We will test 2-3 competitor university sites (selected based on geographic and programmatic overlap) with the same task protocol, establishing a baseline for "what good looks like" in the minds of Holy Family's prospective students.

### Competitive Analysis

We will analyze the digital presence of 6-8 peer and aspirational institutions selected collaboratively with your enrollment and marketing teams. The analysis covers:

- Information architecture patterns (how programs are organized, how audiences are segmented)
- Conversion flow design (RFI forms, application entry points, virtual tour integration)
- Content strategy (program page depth, outcome data presentation, faculty visibility)
- Technical performance (Core Web Vitals, mobile experience, accessibility posture)
- AI search visibility (how competitor institutions appear in ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Google AI Overviews for key queries like "nursing programs in Philadelphia" or "Catholic universities near me")

The output is a Competitive Landscape Matrix that identifies gaps Holy Family can exploit and standards it must meet.

### Content Strategy

The content audit is not a separate workstream -- it is the foundation of the entire project. Holy Family's current 1,601 URLs include approximately 670 news articles (42% of the sitemap), 20+ duplicative request-information forms scattered across individual program pages, and 180+ top-level URL sections reflecting years of ungoverned departmental publishing.

Our content strategy process:

1. **Quantitative inventory:** Every URL cataloged with page type, last modified date, traffic volume, inbound link count, and content owner.
2. **AI-assisted classification:** We use batch LLM processing to categorize each page (keep, rewrite, consolidate, archive, delete), flag duplicate content, and identify pages with no inbound links or traffic. This accelerates what would otherwise be weeks of manual triage.
3. **Content decision tree:** Each page enters a documented decision framework:
 - **Keep as-is** -- current, accurate, well-trafficked, structurally sound
 - **Rewrite** -- strategically important but poorly written, missing outcomes data, or not optimized for search
 - **Consolidate** -- multiple pages covering the same topic merged into a single authoritative page (e.g., the 20+ request-information forms become a single intelligent form with program routing)
 - **Archive** -- historically valuable but no longer current (news articles older than 2 years, past event pages, expired campaign content)
 - **Delete** -- test pages, seasonal greetings, duplicate URLs, broken content
4. **Content model definition:** Structured templates for each content type (program pages, faculty profiles, news articles, events, policies) with required fields, optional fields, and editorial guidelines. This is how consistency survives staff turnover.
5. **Content governance plan:** Ownership assignments, update cadences, approval workflows, and archival triggers for every content type. This is the deliverable that prevents Holy Family from returning to 1,600 ungoverned pages within three years.

## B.3 Information Architecture Approach

### The Multi-Audience Challenge

Holy Family's current site attempts to serve every audience from every page, resulting in navigation that serves no audience well. The RFP identifies this directly: "overly complex, users can't find things."

Our IA approach solves this through audience-aware pathways layered on top of a clean content hierarchy, rather than building separate audience silos that duplicate content.

**Proposed Primary Architecture (8-12 sections replacing 180+):**

| Section | Primary Audience | Content |
|---|---|---|
| `/programs/` | Prospective students | All academic programs with structured data, organized by school, level, and format |
| `/admissions/` | Prospective students | Application process, financial aid, campus visits, single smart RFI form |
| `/student-life/` | Prospective + current students | Housing, activities, campus ministry, resources |
| `/about/` | All audiences | Leadership, mission, history, news, accreditation |
| `/news/` | All audiences | Current news and Bold Ideas blog (2-year rolling archive) |
| `/athletics/` | Students + community | Sports programs, schedules, recruitment |
| `/alumni/` | Alumni + donors | Engagement, giving, reunions |
| `/resources/` | Current students + faculty | IT services, library, career services, registrar |
| `/directory/` | All audiences | Faculty and staff profiles with department filtering |

**Audience entry points:** Rather than building separate "prospective student" and "current student" portals that create content duplication, we will implement audience-specific landing pages that curate pathways into the shared content structure. A prospective undergraduate arriving from a paid search campaign sees a hero focused on program discovery and campus visit scheduling. A current student arriving from a bookmarked link sees quick access to registration, the library, and student services. The underlying content is the same; the entry experience is personalized.

### IA Validation

We do not ship information architecture based on internal logic alone. Our validation process includes:

- **Card sorting** (open and closed) with 15-20 participants across audience segments to test whether our proposed groupings match user mental models
- **Tree testing** on the proposed navigation structure to measure task completion rates before any visual design is applied
- **First-click testing** on key tasks (find the Nursing program requirements, apply for financial aid, request information about the MBA) to validate that the primary navigation labels work
- **Comparison testing** against the current IA to establish measurable improvement benchmarks

### Navigation Design

- **Primary navigation:** 6-8 top-level items with structured mega-menus that expose program categories and key conversion paths without requiring multiple clicks
- **Utility navigation:** Audience-specific quick links (Apply, Visit, Request Info for prospects; MyHFU portal, Email, Library for current students)
- **Contextual navigation:** Within program sections, persistent sidebar or breadcrumb navigation showing related programs, faculty, and next steps
- **Search:** Prominent, fast, and intelligent -- surfacing programs, people, and pages with type-ahead suggestions (detailed in our development approach)
- **Mobile navigation:** Full-featured bottom navigation with thumb-zone optimization, not a buried hamburger menu that hides the site's most important pathways

## B.4 Design Process

### Research-Driven UX

Our design process begins with evidence, not mood boards. Before any visual design work begins, we will have completed:

- Stakeholder interviews establishing brand positioning and enrollment priorities
- Usability testing on the current site identifying specific friction points
- Analytics analysis revealing actual user behavior patterns
- IA validation confirming the structural framework

This means our designers are solving documented problems, not guessing at aesthetic preferences.

### Design System Philosophy

Rick Mitchell has already invested in building a design system at Holy Family. We will not discard that work. Our approach is to audit the existing system, identify what translates to the new WordPress environment, and extend it into a comprehensive, documented component library that serves both the website build and ongoing content creation.

The design system will include:

- **Design tokens:** Colors, typography, spacing, shadows, and motion values defined as reusable variables that ensure consistency across every page and component
- **Component library:** 40-60 production-ready components (buttons, cards, forms, navigation elements, content blocks, media players, CTAs, testimonial displays, data tables, accordions, tabs, hero patterns) documented with usage guidelines, accessibility specifications, and responsive behavior
- **Page templates:** Defined layouts for each content type (program page, faculty profile, news article, landing page, event page) with required and optional component slots
- **Brand integration:** Holy Family's visual identity -- the purple and gold palette, the university seal, the Newtown and Northeast Philadelphia campus photography -- embedded as design tokens so that brand consistency is structural, not dependent on individual designer judgment
- **Figma handoff:** A complete Figma component library mirroring the production code, enabling Rick's team to design new pages using the same system we built

### From Wireframes to Production

**Low-fidelity wireframes (Phase 2, week 1):** Grayscale layouts for 8-10 key page templates focused on content hierarchy, component placement, and conversion flow. No color, no imagery -- this is where we validate structure without aesthetic distraction.

**High-fidelity mockups (Phase 2, weeks 2-3):** Full visual design applied to approved wireframes. Brand colors, typography, photography, and micro-interactions. We present 2 design directions for the homepage and 1 direction for interior pages, all built from the same component system.

**Interactive prototypes (Phase 2, week 3):** Clickable prototypes for the 3 most critical user journeys (program discovery to RFI submission, admissions information to application start, homepage to campus visit request). These prototypes are tested with 5-8 users before development begins.

**Design QA (ongoing during development):** Our designers review every implemented component against the approved mockups, flagging deviations before they compound. Design QA is not a phase -- it is a continuous checkpoint.

### Mobile-First, Not Mobile-Adapted

Over 60% of prospective student traffic arrives on mobile devices. We design for the small screen first and enhance for larger viewports, rather than designing for desktop and then compressing.

This means:

- Touch targets meet the WCAG 2.2 minimum of 24x24 CSS pixels
- Form fields are optimized for mobile keyboards (tel input for phone numbers, email input for addresses)
- Critical conversion paths (Request Info, Apply, Visit) are reachable within one thumb tap from any page
- Image and video assets are responsive and lazy-loaded, with explicit width/height attributes to prevent layout shift
- Content is authored mobile-first: if a content block does not work on a phone screen, it does not ship

## B.5 Development Methodology

### Technical Standards

We build on WordPress using modern development practices that produce maintainable, performant, secure code:

- **WordPress Coding Standards** (WPCS) enforced via PHPCS linting in the CI pipeline -- every committed file passes automated style and security checks
- **Semantic HTML5** as the structural foundation -- proper heading hierarchy, landmark regions, native form elements, and ARIA attributes only where native semantics are insufficient
- **CSS architecture** using a utility-first methodology (Tailwind or custom design tokens compiled to utility classes) with BEM naming for custom components -- no specificity wars, no `!important` overrides
- **JavaScript** kept minimal and progressive -- core functionality works without JS; enhanced interactions (mega-menu animations, form validation, lazy loading) layer on top
- **PHP 8.2+** with strict typing where applicable, following WordPress Plugin API conventions for all custom functionality
- **Git-based workflow** on Pantheon: feature branches, pull requests with mandatory code review, automated testing on merge, promotion through Dev/Test/Live environments

### Quality Assurance

QA is not a phase at the end of the project. It is embedded in every sprint:

- **Automated testing pipeline:**
 - PHP linting (PHPCS with WordPress-Extra ruleset)
 - Accessibility linting (axe-core integrated into CI -- every pull request is scanned)
 - Visual regression testing (Percy or BackstopJS comparing screenshots across browsers and viewports)
 - Performance budgets (Lighthouse CI enforcing thresholds: Performance > 90, Accessibility > 95, Best Practices > 90)
 - Link checking (automated crawl on staging to catch 404s before promotion)

- **Manual testing protocol:**
 - Cross-browser testing on Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge (current and current-1 versions)
 - Device testing on iOS Safari, Android Chrome, and a representative tablet viewport
 - Screen reader testing (VoiceOver on macOS/iOS, NVDA on Windows) for all interactive components and form flows
 - Keyboard-only navigation testing for every page template

- **Code review process:**
 - Every pull request requires review by a senior developer before merge
 - Reviews check for: security vulnerabilities (SQL injection, XSS, CSRF), performance regressions, accessibility compliance, adherence to the component library, and code maintainability
 - Rick Mitchell and his team will have read access to the repository throughout development and are invited to participate in code reviews -- this is part of knowledge transfer, not a courtesy

### Performance Optimization

University websites lose prospective students to slow load times. Our performance targets:

| Metric | Target | Method |
|---|---|---|
| Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) | < 2.5s | Image optimization, CDN delivery, critical CSS inlining |
| First Input Delay (FID) | < 100ms | Minimal JS, deferred non-critical scripts, no render-blocking third-party tags |
| Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) | < 0.1 | Explicit image dimensions, font-display swap, stable component layouts |
| Time to First Byte (TTFB) | < 600ms | Pantheon's global CDN, Redis object caching, optimized database queries |
| Total page weight | < 1.5MB | WebP/AVIF images, code splitting, lazy loading below the fold |

These targets are enforced in CI. A pull request that degrades Lighthouse performance scores below threshold cannot merge.

## B.6 Content Strategy & Migration

### Migration at Scale

Migrating 1,601 URLs from Drupal to WordPress is not a manual copy-paste exercise. It is a structured, tool-assisted process with human quality gates at every stage.

**Migration toolchain:**

1. **FG Drupal to WordPress Premium** -- reads the Drupal database directly and migrates articles, pages, taxonomies, media, and URL structures into WordPress. Handles the bulk transfer of content nodes, preserving metadata and relationships.
2. **WP All Import** -- handles refinement passes, mapping migrated content into WordPress Custom Post Types (Programs, Faculty, Events) with proper ACF field assignments.
3. **AI-assisted content processing** -- batch LLM processing to clean legacy HTML, generate missing meta descriptions, write alt text for untagged images, normalize formatting inconsistencies, and flag content that needs human rewriting. This is not AI generating content for Holy Family. It is AI accelerating the mechanical cleanup work so our writers focus on strategic content.
4. **Custom migration scripts** -- for the 5-10% of content that resists automated tooling (deeply nested Drupal Paragraphs, complex entity references, embedded media relationships).
5. **WP-CLI + Terminus** -- command-line tools for scripted batch operations on the Pantheon WordPress environment.

**Migration sequence:**

- Phase 1: Content audit and consolidation decisions (what moves, what merges, what archives)
- Phase 2: Content model build (WordPress CPTs, ACF field groups, taxonomies)
- Phase 3: Bulk automated migration (FG Drupal + WP All Import)
- Phase 4: AI-assisted cleanup pass (HTML normalization, metadata generation, formatting)
- Phase 5: Manual quality review (human editors verify every migrated program page, admissions page, and top-50 traffic page)
- Phase 6: Redirect map validation (every old URL tested against the new URL)

### Content Audit Methodology

Our audit will classify every page in the current sitemap:

| Classification | Criteria | Estimated Count |
|---|---|---|
| **Migrate and optimize** | High-traffic, enrollment-critical, or strategically important | ~400 pages |
| **Migrate as-is** | Current, accurate, adequate content | ~400 pages |
| **Consolidate** | Multiple pages on same topic merged into one (especially the 20+ standalone RFI forms) | ~200 pages reduced to ~50 |
| **Archive** | News older than 2 years, past events, expired campaigns | ~400 pages |
| **Delete** | Test pages, seasonal content, broken/empty pages | ~100 pages |

The result: approximately 850-1,000 active pages in the new site, down from 1,601 -- a leaner, faster, more maintainable website that serves users better because there is less noise between them and the content they need.

### SEO, AEO, and GEO Optimization

Traditional SEO is necessary but no longer sufficient. Half of prospective students now use AI tools weekly to research programs. 93% of AI search sessions end without a click to a website -- the content AI surfaces from your site IS the first impression a prospective student receives. When AI users do click through, they convert at 4.4x the rate of traditional organic visitors. Holy Family must be visible and authoritative in both traditional search and AI-mediated discovery.

**Search Engine Optimization (SEO):**

- Technical SEO foundation: clean URL structure, XML sitemaps, canonical tags, structured internal linking, optimized robots.txt
- On-page optimization for every program page: unique title tags, meta descriptions, heading hierarchy, image alt text
- Content depth: program pages that function as the definitive answer to "What is the [X] program at Holy Family University?" -- including enrollment numbers, graduation rates, employment outcomes with salary data, tuition per credit, student-faculty ratio, accreditations, and named student testimonials
- Validated 301 redirect map covering every active URL, generated via semantic similarity matching (embedding-based automated mapping with human review for matches below 0.98 confidence) and tested pre-launch

**AI Engine Optimization (AEO):**

- **Structured data (Schema.org)** on every page type:
 - `EducationalOrganization` on the homepage
 - `EducationalOccupationalProgram` on every program page (name, provider, cost, duration, occupational category, salary upon completion)
 - `FAQPage` on admissions, financial aid, and student life pages -- FAQ schema has the highest citation probability in AI-generated responses
 - `Event` for open houses and recruitment events
 - `Article` and `BlogPosting` on news content
- **Content architecture for citability:** Pages updated within 2 months receive significantly more AI citations than stale content. Content that includes specific statistics, named sources, and direct quotes earns 30-40% higher AI visibility. We will structure program pages to be data-rich and regularly refreshable.
- **Reading level optimization:** AI engines preferentially cite content written at a grade 6-8 reading level. Our content guidelines will specify clear, direct prose without academic jargon on public-facing pages.
- **`llms.txt` protocol:** We will place a machine-readable Markdown file at the domain root (`/llms.txt`) listing Holy Family's key institutional pages -- program directories, admissions information, tuition and financial aid facts, application deadlines. This is a low-effort, high-signal implementation that demonstrates technical sophistication.

**Generative Engine Optimization (GEO):**

- Monitor Holy Family's appearance in ChatGPT, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews, and Bing Copilot for key enrollment queries
- Establish canonical fact pages for tuition, deadlines, admission requirements, and program outcomes -- single authoritative sources that reduce the risk of AI engines synthesizing conflicting information from multiple Holy Family URLs
- Monthly content freshness updates on high-priority pages to maintain citation recency signals

### URL Structure

The new URL structure will be clean, hierarchical, and permanent:

 

Every current URL will have a corresponding 301 redirect. We will deliver a validated redirect map covering all 1,601 URLs as a project deliverable, tested both programmatically (automated crawl comparison) and manually (spot-checking the top 100 traffic pages and all externally linked pages).
