## B.7 Stakeholder Management

### The Core Challenge

University websites involve more content stakeholders than almost any other type of project. At Holy Family, four academic Schools, enrollment management, student affairs, campus ministry, athletics, advancement, IT, the president's office, and marketing all have legitimate content needs and strong opinions about how the website should represent their work. Left unmanaged, this produces scope creep, design-by-committee, and a website that tries to say everything and communicates nothing.

### Our Framework: Structured Input, Clear Authority

**Steering Committee (5-7 members)**
Meets biweekly. Includes the VP for Marketing and Communications, Director of Digital Experience, VP for Enrollment Management, a representative academic dean (rotating), and VP for Institutional Effectiveness. This group makes binding decisions on scope, timeline, design direction, and content priorities. Decisions documented in writing within 24 hours.

**Content Working Groups (by School/Division)**
Each of the four Schools and each major administrative division (enrollment, student life, advancement, campus ministry) designates a single content liaison. Content liaisons attend monthly working sessions where they review content templates, provide department-specific content, and validate program information accuracy. They do not attend design reviews or make architectural decisions.

**Decision Escalation Protocol**
When two stakeholders disagree, the disagreement is documented with both positions and escalated to the Steering Committee at the next meeting (or within 48 hours if blocking). The Steering Committee decides. This protocol is established in writing at project kickoff and referenced throughout -- it prevents hallway conversations from overriding structured decisions.

**Feedback Windows**
Each design and content milestone has a defined feedback window (typically 5 business days). Feedback submitted within the window is incorporated. Feedback submitted after the window is documented for a future phase. This prevents the project from entering an infinite revision loop while respecting every stakeholder's right to be heard.

### Consensus Building

We have found that stakeholder conflict in higher education usually stems from fear of invisibility -- departments worry that the redesign will bury their content. We address this proactively:

- **Content visibility audit:** Early in discovery, we show each department where their content currently lives, how much traffic it receives, and where it will live in the new IA. This replaces anxiety with data.
- **Template demonstrations:** Before asking departments to commit content to templates, we show populated examples using their actual content. Seeing "this is what your program page will look like" resolves more objections than any amount of wireframe explanation.
- **Shared metrics dashboard:** Post-launch, every content owner sees how their pages perform (views, engagement, conversion) through a shared GA4 dashboard. This shifts the conversation from "is my content visible?" to "is my content working?"

## B.9 Risk Management

### Identified Risks and Mitigation Strategies

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation | Contingency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Content delivery delays from departments** | High | High -- blocks migration and testing | Content liaisons assigned per department at kickoff; content templates with required fields delivered in Week 4; biweekly content deadline reminders with Steering Committee escalation for non-response | Placeholder content in staging allows development to continue; delayed content enters post-launch optimization queue |
| **Drupal Paragraphs complexity** | Medium | High -- can slow migration by 2-4 weeks | Pre-migration Drupal audit to catalog all Paragraph types and nesting patterns; Gutenberg block mapping decided before migration begins, not during | Reserve 80 hours in the migration budget for manual Paragraph conversion if automated tooling fails on deeply nested structures |
| **Scope creep from stakeholder requests** | High | Medium -- erodes timeline and budget | Structured feedback windows; Steering Committee authority; documented scope with change order process for additions | Phase 2 backlog absorbs legitimate requests that surface after design approval without disrupting the launch timeline |
| **Slate CRM integration dependencies** | Medium | Medium -- form submissions are enrollment-critical | Gravity Forms webhook integration (fully WordPress-native) eliminates dependency on Slate's embed code and CSS conflicts; Ping tracking script implemented via GTM on day one of development | If Slate API access is delayed by IT, forms function with email notification as interim while webhook integration is completed |
| **ADA/WCAG compliance gaps in legacy content** | High | High -- legal exposure since the April 24, 2024 Title II deadline has passed | Accessibility built into every phase: design tokens with 4.5:1 contrast ratios, axe-core in CI pipeline, screen reader testing on all components, Siteimprove continuous monitoring | PDF remediation scoped as a separate, ongoing engagement rather than blocking the website launch; VPAT/ACR delivered with the site to document compliance posture |
| **SEO traffic loss during migration** | Medium | High -- organic traffic is a primary enrollment channel | Semantic 301 redirect map covering all 1,601 URLs, validated programmatically and manually; XML sitemap submitted to Google Search Console on launch day; 90-day post-launch SEO monitoring with weekly ranking reports | If traffic drops exceed 15% on any critical program page, we conduct immediate technical audit and redirect correction within 48 hours |
| **Third-party integration failures** (Slate, payment systems, LMS, directory sync) | Low | High -- can block critical functionality | Integration requirements documented and confirmed during discovery; sandbox testing environments for every integration; integration testing begins mid-Phase 3, in parallel with template assembly, not deferred to QA | Graceful degradation: forms fall back to email submission, directory falls back to static content, event feeds fall back to manual entry |
| **Timeline compression due to delayed kickoff** | Medium | Medium -- reduces testing and content review time | Critical path identified at kickoff with float built into content review phases; phased launch option (core site live by target date, secondary content migrated in Phase 2) | MVP launch covering homepage, program pages, admissions flow, and top-50 traffic pages meets the enrollment marketing calendar even if secondary content migration extends 4-6 weeks |

### Ongoing Risk Monitoring

We maintain a project risk register reviewed at every biweekly Steering Committee meeting. Risks are rated on likelihood and impact, with assigned owners and due dates for mitigation actions. The register is a living document, not a proposal artifact -- new risks identified during the project are added and tracked with the same rigor.

## C.1 CMS Recommendation: WordPress

We recommend WordPress as the content management system for Holy Family University's redesigned website. Not headless WordPress. Not a decoupled architecture. Traditional, full-stack WordPress -- the same platform that powers 43% of the web and the majority of higher education institutions that have migrated away from Drupal in the past five years.

Drupal's editorial tools have improved, but WordPress remains the benchmark for content author experience. When your web team is measured in single digits, the CMS must work for them, not against them.
